text:
Traffic flow prediction is a crucial part of Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITS) and has significant practical value. However, there are many challenges in designing a suitable model for traffic data due to its complex and ever-changing spatial-temporal relationships in urban roads, which cannot be fully captured by small-scale or static models. Spatial-Temporal Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) are widely used to model the underlying patterns of traffic data. However, there are three main challenges: (1) static spatial modeling may not be able to capture the dynamic movement trend in traffic data; (2) the local neighboring interactions in current graph-based models cannot fully exploit the global and far-away but similar relationships between distant regions, which is very common in the urban area such as different interchanges, same road with different names, etc.; (3) it does not consider the communication latency between regions, which is also an important factor for traffic modeling.

Existing graph neural network-based traffic forecasting approaches are severely restricted by large variations in propagation delay and complex temporal spatial evolutions in real-world road networks. In this work, we propose a novel graph neural network framework named PDFormer, standing for Propagation Delay-aware dynamic long-range Transformer. The PDFormer framework employs a spatial self-attention mechanism that adaptively captures the changing spatial relationships over time, and uses two graph masking matrices to respectively emphasize the short physical distance and the long-term semantic correlation between nodes. Additionally, a traffic delay-aware feature transformation mechanism is introduced to capture the time delay caused by the different information propagation speeds. Experiment results on six real-world datasets validate that the proposed PDFormer model not only surpasses the previous SOTA models in forecasting accuracy but also boasts high computational speed. More importantly, through the visualization of learned attention weights, we obtain insights into the inner workings of the proposed model and deeply understand how it extracts informative patterns from complex and dynamic traffic environments.
